---
title: "Align features to a timeline"
subtitle: "Use `ExtractFeaturesResult.align_to()` before joining or modeling rows."
sidebar: cookbook
---

## Use when

You have extracted more than one feature type and want a clear mapping between them. Common examples are dense language features aligned to words, audio features aligned to events, or visual features aligned to video frames.

## Text features aligned to word events

```{python}
from natural_features.workflows import extract_features

result = extract_features(
    "alpha beta beta",
    features=["text.tokenize", "language.surface"],
)

aligned = result.align_to(
    "text.tokenize",
    features="language.surface",
)

alignment_table = aligned.to_dataframe()
alignment_table.loc[
    :,
    [
        "source_name",
        "source_row",
        "source_onset_s",
        "source_offset_s",
        "source_reference",
        "mapped_onset_s",
        "target_reference",
        "target_index_start",
        "target_index_end",
        "target_time_s",
    ],
]
```

The source feature remains a `FeatureSeries`; the target is a `Timeline` derived from `text.tokenize`.

```{python}
{
    "source_type": type(result.features["language.surface"]).__name__,
    "target_kind": aligned.target.kind,
    "alignment_type": type(aligned.alignments["language.surface"]).__name__,
}
```

## Annotate events when that is useful

Event outputs can receive alignment columns because their row shape is already event-like.

```{python}
word_aligned = result.align_to(
    "text.tokenize",
    features="text.tokenize",
).annotated_features(prefix="word")

word_aligned["text.tokenize"].extra["word_index_start"].tolist()
```

Dense feature outputs are not rewritten into events. Use `FeatureAlignment.to_dataframe()` when you want a rectangular join surface.

## Video features aligned to frames

`VideoStimulus` inputs automatically provide a `video_frames` timeline. This example uses synthetic frames, so it does not need ffmpeg or model downloads.

```{python}
import numpy as np

from natural_features.core.stimulus import VideoStimulus
from natural_features.workflows import extract_features

frames = np.zeros((4, 12, 12, 3), dtype=np.float32)
frames[:, 3:8, 4:9, 0] = np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 4)[:, None, None]
video = VideoStimulus.from_array(frames, fps=2.0)

video_result = extract_features(video, features=["vision.energy"])
frame_aligned = video_result.align_to("video_frames", features="vision.energy")

frame_aligned.to_dataframe().loc[
    :,
    [
        "source_name",
        "source_row",
        "source_onset_s",
        "target_index_start",
        "target_time_s",
    ],
]
```

## Boundary it closes

Alignment is not a table merge. It is a recorded mapping between a source
feature object's time coordinates and a target timeline. Source and target
clock references are retained; when they differ, alignment requires a
resolvable `TemporalContext` and records the converted coordinates. It never
interpolates feature values. Export to a table after the mapping exists.

## See also

[Unified workflow](../concepts/unified-workflow.qmd) · [Native-grid handoff](native-grid-handoff.qmd) · [Five feature types, five concepts](five-feature-concepts.qmd) · [Feature table merge](feature-table-merge.qmd)
